input: Convert this MKV to H.264 MP4 with AAC audio
stdout:
ffmpeg -i input.mkv -map 0:v:0 -map 0:a:0 -c:v libx264 -preset medium -crf 23 -c:a aac -b:a 128k output.mp4

- Maps the first video and first audio stream explicitly to avoid ambiguity with multi-track sources
- Encodes video as H.264 using libx264 with CRF 23 (visually transparent quality) and medium preset for balanced speed/compression
- Encodes audio as AAC at 128 kbit/s stereo, standard for MP4 delivery
- Use ffprobe input.mkv to verify stream indices if the file has multiple video or audio tracks

ffmpeg -i input.mkv -map 0:a:0 -c:a pcm_s24le -ar 48000 output.wav

- Extracts the first audio stream (-map 0:a:0) to uncompressed 24-bit PCM WAV
- Uses pcm_s24le codec for 24-bit little-endian audio at 48kHz sample rate
- Verify input audio track index with ffprobe if your source has multiple audio streams
- WAV container requires explicit codec; defaults would fall back to 16-bit

input: Scale to 1920x1080, keep aspect ratio, pad with black
stdout:
ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -vf "scale=1920:1080:force_original_aspect_ratio=decrease,pad=1920:1080:(ow-iw)/2:(oh-ih)/2:color=black" -c:v libx264 -preset medium -crf 23 -c:a copy output.mp4

- scale filter resizes to fit within 1920x1080 while preserving aspect ratio (decrease ensures no dimension exceeds target)
- pad filter adds black bars centered horizontally and vertically to fill exact 1920x1080 frame
- Video re-encoded with H.264 at CRF 23 (adjust for quality/size tradeoff); audio copied without re-encoding
- Verify input resolution with ffprobe first to confirm scaling behavior matches expectations

ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -af loudnorm=I=-23:TP=-1:LRA=7 -c:v copy -c:a aac -b:a 192k output.mp4

- Applies single-pass loudnorm filter targeting -23 LUFS (EBU R128 broadcast standard) with -1 dBTP true peak and 7 LU loudness range
- Copies video stream unchanged, re-encodes audio to AAC at 192k
- This is approximate; for accurate normalization, use a two-pass workflow: first pass measures loudness stats, second pass applies precise correction based on measured values
- Verify final loudness with ffmpeg -i output.mp4 -af loudnorm=print_format=summary -f null - or use tools like loudness-scanner

ffmpeg -f concat -safe 0 -i filelist.txt -c copy output.mp4

- Creates a filelist.txt containing lines like "file 'video1.mp4'" for each input in order
- Uses the concat demuxer to join segments without re-encoding (-c copy)
- Requires all inputs to have identical codec/resolution/framerate for stream copy to work
- Verify track compatibility with ffprobe beforehand; mismatches require re-encoding with concat filter instead

input: Remux only — copy all streams, change container to MP4
stdout:
ffmpeg -i input.mkv -map 0 -c copy output.mp4

- Copies all streams (video, audio, subtitles, data) without re-encoding
- -map 0 includes every stream from the input file
- -c copy uses stream copy mode for maximum speed and zero quality loss
- Note: MP4 container may not support all subtitle formats (e.g., PGS, ASS); verify with ffprobe if streams fail to copy

ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -vf subtitles=subtitles.srt -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -preset medium -c:a copy output.mp4

- Burns the SRT subtitle file directly into the video frames using the subtitles filter
- Re-encodes video with libx264 at CRF 18 (high quality) to preserve visual fidelity during the burn-in process
- Copies the audio stream without re-encoding to avoid quality loss
- Outputs to MP4 container suitable for web and broadcast delivery

ffmpeg -ss 00:01:30 -i input.mkv -frames:v 1 -q:v 1 output.png

- `-ss 00:01:30` seeks to 1 minute 30 seconds before decoding (fast input seeking)
- `-frames:v 1` extracts exactly one video frame
- `-q:v 1` sets PNG quality to highest (lowest compression, best fidelity)
- Output format is PNG by extension; frame is extracted at the seek point

ffmpeg -i input.mts -vf yadif=1:-1:0 -c:v libx264 -preset medium -crf 18 -c:a copy output.mp4

- Uses yadif deinterlacer with mode=1 (output one frame per field, doubling framerate from 29.97i to 59.94p or 25i to 50p)
- Encodes to H.264 with CRF 18 for high quality; adjust CRF lower for better quality or higher for smaller files
- Copies audio unchanged; use ffprobe to verify field order if results look wrong (yadif auto-detects by default)
- For broadcast: consider mode=0 (one frame per frame, same framerate) if 59.94p/50p is not desired

ffmpeg -i input.mkv -c:v prores_ks -profile:v 3 -c:a pcm_s24le -ar 48000 output.mov

- Encodes video to ProRes 422 HQ (profile 3) using the prores_ks encoder
- Converts audio to 24-bit PCM at 48kHz, standard for broadcast workflows
- Outputs to MOV container, the native wrapper for ProRes
- Verify input stream indices with ffprobe if multiple tracks exist; adjust -map parameters as needed

ffmpeg -i input.mxf -c:v prores_ks -profile:v 3 -vendor ap10 -pix_fmt yuv422p10le -color_primaries bt709 -color_trc bt709 -colorspace bt709 -c:a pcm_s24le -ar 48000 -map 0:v:0 -map 0:a:0 output.mxf

- Creates a broadcast-grade MXF master (ProRes 422 HQ video, 24-bit PCM audio) suitable as an IMF track file placeholder.
- Sets Rec.709 color metadata and 48kHz audio to match typical IMF delivery specs.
- This is NOT a complete IMF package—real IMF requires CPL, ASSETMAP, PKL, and potentially multiple track files conforming to specific Application specs.
- Use this as a starting point for individual essence files; authoring a full IMP requires specialized IMF packaging tools beyond FFmpeg.

input: H.264 CRF 18, slow preset, web streaming
stdout:
ffmpeg -i input.mkv -c:v libx264 -preset slow -crf 18 -c:a aac -b:a 192k -movflags +faststart output.mp4

- H.264 video at CRF 18 (visually lossless quality) with slow preset for better compression efficiency
- AAC audio at 192k for web compatibility
- +faststart flag relocates moov atom to file start, enabling progressive streaming/playback before full download
- Output to MP4 container for broad web player support

input: Strip all audio, keep video only
stdout:
ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -map 0:v -c:v copy -an output.mp4

- Maps only the video stream(s) from the input using `-map 0:v`
- Copies the video codec directly without re-encoding via `-c:v copy` for speed and quality preservation
- Explicitly removes all audio with `-an` (no audio)
- Outputs a video-only MP4 file suitable for web delivery or further processing

input: Two audio tracks — map English AAC, copy video
stdout:
ffmpeg -i input.mkv -map 0:v:0 -c:v copy -map 0:a:0 -map 0:a:1 -c:a aac -b:a 192k output.mp4

- Maps the first video stream and copies it without re-encoding
- Maps the first two audio streams and encodes both to AAC at 192k per track
- Assumes the first two audio tracks are the desired English streams; verify track order with `ffprobe input.mkv` and adjust indices if needed
- If language metadata is reliable, use `-map 0:a:m:language:eng` to select only English tracks automatically

ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -vf "crop=ih*16/9:ih:(iw-ih*16/9)/2:0" -c:a copy output.mp4

- Crops a centered 16:9 region from a 4:3 source by calculating width as (height × 16/9) and centering horizontally.
- X-offset formula (iw-ih*16/9)/2 ensures the crop is centered; Y-offset is 0 (no vertical crop needed).
- Audio is stream-copied; re-encode video with your desired codec/quality settings if needed (add -c:v libx264 -crf 18, etc.).

INPUT:
- in.mp4: source video file

VIDEO FILTERING:
- scale=1920:1080: resizes video to 1920x1080 (Full HD)
- Aspect ratio not preserved unless source matches 16:9
- May introduce distortion if source aspect differs

VIDEO ENCODING:
- libx264: H.264 codec (re-encodes video)
- Default preset (medium) and CRF (~23) apply
- Quality loss from re-encoding

AUDIO HANDLING:
- No explicit audio codec specified
- FFmpeg auto-selects encoder (likely AAC for MP4)
- Audio is re-encoded (not stream copied)

OUTPUT:
- out.mp4: output container

KEY RISKS:
- Forced scaling may distort image if aspect ratio mismatches
- Full re-encode of both streams (slower, quality loss)
- No bitrate/quality control specified (relies on defaults)

COMMON ALTERNATIVES:
- Add -c:a copy to avoid re-encoding audio
- Use scale=1920:-1 to preserve aspect ratio
- Add -crf 18 for higher quality or -preset faster for speed

**Input/Output:**
- Input: in.mov (QuickTime container)
- Output: out.mp4 (MP4 container)

**Processing:**
- `-c copy` — Stream copy mode for all streams (video, audio, subtitles)
- No re-encoding; copies compressed data directly from container to container
- Fast operation (near-instant for small files)
- Output quality identical to input

**Key Considerations:**
- Requires codec compatibility: MP4 must support the codecs present in the MOV file (usually fine for H.264/AAC)
- Will fail if source contains codecs unsupported by MP4 (e.g., ProRes video without conversion)
- Metadata/timecodes may shift slightly during remuxing
- No quality loss since no transcoding occurs

Input:
- "-i in.mp4" – source file (MP4 container, likely video + audio)

Output configuration:
- "-vn" – disables video stream (no video in output)
- "-acodec pcm_s24le" – encodes audio to uncompressed PCM, 24-bit signed little-endian
- "out.wav" – output file (WAV container)

Stream handling:
- Only audio is extracted and re-encoded
- Video is discarded entirely

Key points:
- Full audio re-encode (not stream copy), introduces processing overhead
- 24-bit PCM produces large, lossless files (~4.6 MB per minute stereo at 48 kHz)
- Sample rate and channel layout preserved from source by default
- WAV container does not support metadata or chapters

Risk considerations:
- If source audio is already lossless or high bitrate, consider "-acodec copy" if container supports it
- No quality loss during conversion, but file size will be significantly larger than compressed formats

Input:
- `-f concat`: Uses the concat demuxer to read a list of files
- `-safe 0`: Disables safe mode, allowing absolute paths in list.txt
- `-i list.txt`: Text file containing file paths (format: "file '/path/to/video.mp4'")

Processing:
- `-c copy`: Stream copy mode—no re-encoding (video/audio copied as-is)

Output:
- `out.mp4`: Concatenated result in MP4 container

Key Points:
- All input files must have identical codec parameters (resolution, framerate, codec, etc.)
- Extremely fast since no encoding occurs
- If files differ in specs, you'll get errors or broken output
- list.txt format: one line per file as `file 'path'`

Risks:
- Mismatched codecs/formats will fail silently or produce corrupted output
- Safe mode disabled means any file path is allowed (potential security concern in automated systems)

INPUT:
• -i in.mp4 — Single input file

FILTER COMPLEX:
• [0:v]yadif[outv] — Deinterlaces video stream from input 0 using yadif (Yet Another Deinterlacing Filter)
• Labels output as [outv] for later mapping

STREAM MAPPING:
• -map "[outv]" — Maps deinterlaced video from filtergraph to output
• -map 0:a — Maps all audio streams from input 0 to output (bypasses filtergraph)

ENCODING:
• -c:v libx264 — Re-encodes video using H.264 codec (required because filtergraph modifies video)
• -c:a copy — Stream copies audio without re-encoding (no processing applied)

OUTPUT:
• out.mp4 — MP4 container with deinterlaced video and original audio

KEY NOTES:
• Video must be re-encoded because yadif alters frame structure
• Audio remains untouched (fast, lossless passthrough)
• Default libx264 settings used (medium preset, CRF 23 typical)
• If input is already progressive, yadif adds minimal overhead but won't harm output

**Input:**
- `-i in.mp4` – Single input file

**Stream Selection:**
- `-map 0:v:0` – Select first video stream from input 0
- `-map 0:a:1` – Select second audio stream from input 0
- Explicit mapping overrides default stream selection; only mapped streams are included in output

**Codec Settings:**
- `-c copy` – Stream copy mode (no re-encoding) for all mapped streams
- Fast operation, no quality loss
- Output container must support the source codecs/formats

**Output:**
- `out.mp4` – Single output file with 1 video + 1 audio stream

**Key Notes:**
- If input has only one audio stream, `-map 0:a:1` will fail (stream not found)
- Stream copy preserves timestamps, metadata, and bitstream exactly
- Useful for extracting specific tracks or dropping unwanted streams without re-encoding

**Input:**
- `-i in.mp4` — Single video input file

**Audio Filter:**
- `-af loudnorm=I=-23:TP=-1:LRA=7` — EBU R128 loudness normalization
  - `I=-23` — Integrated loudness target of -23 LUFS (broadcast standard)
  - `TP=-1` — True peak limit of -1 dBTP (prevents clipping)
  - `LRA=7` — Loudness range target of 7 LU (dynamic range compression)
  - Single-pass mode (fast but less accurate; two-pass mode requires measuring first)

**Output:**
- `out.mp4` — Re-encodes both audio and video
  - Audio: Re-encoded with normalization applied
  - Video: Unnecessarily re-encoded (no video processing specified)

**Key Notes:**
- **Video re-encode penalty:** Add `-c:v copy` to stream-copy video and avoid quality loss
- **Single-pass limitation:** Measurement and normalization happen simultaneously; for critical work, measure first with `loudnorm=print_format=json`, then apply measured parameters in second pass
- **No explicit codec selection:** Uses FFmpeg defaults (typically libx264 for video, AAC for audio in MP4)

**Inputs**
- `-i in.mp4`: Single input file

**Hardware Acceleration**
- `-hwaccel cuda`: Enables NVIDIA GPU decoding (applies to input)
- Reduces CPU load during decode, but requires compatible GPU + driver

**Video Codec**
- `-c:v h264_nvenc`: Encodes video using NVIDIA hardware encoder (NVENC)
- H.264 output, GPU-accelerated encoding

**Audio Handling**
- No audio codec specified → default behavior (usually stream copy or re-encode depending on compatibility)

**Output**
- `out.mp4`: MP4 container with hardware-encoded H.264 video

**Key Notes**
- `-hwaccel cuda` placement before `-i` is correct (applies to decoding)
- Full GPU pipeline: decode on GPU → encode on GPU
- No quality/bitrate flags → uses NVENC defaults (may differ from software encoder quality)
- No explicit stream mapping → all streams processed automatically
